Generalizable NeRFs (GeNeRFs) often require image feature extractions that are based on NeRF.
NeRF and its variants simplify the dense 3D reconstruction part of the traditional photogrammetry pipeline by using coordinate-based MLPs for rendering.
NeRF and its variants require known accurate camera poses as inputs for generating high-quality images at novel views.
Most NeRF-related methods obtain camera poses through Structure-from-Motion (SfM) for the rendering process.
BARF applied to GeNeRF leads to quick divergence of camera poses, even with small perturbations in ground truth initialization.
DBARF shows improved results in optimizing camera poses compared to BARF, even with random initialization for camera poses.
DBARF initializes camera poses randomly, which plays a crucial role in the rendering quality of NeRF.
DBARF involves the calculation of keypoint-metric reprojection error in the process of bundle adjustment.
NeRF's rendering quality highly relies on accurate camera poses, which are adjusted jointly with NeRF in works like BARF to enhance performance.
BARF is a representative work that focuses on jointly optimizing camera poses with NeRF to improve rendering quality.
NeRF maps pixel coordinates into high-dimensional space using Fourier features to allow network learning of high-frequency image components.
Fourier features can affect the optimization of camera poses in NeRF, as gradients from high-frequency components dominate during training.
BARF adopts a coarse-to-fine strategy to deal with high-frequency components during training, adjusting camera poses accordingly.
BARF masks out high-frequency components initially and then gradually reintroduces them after stabilizing low-frequency components to optimize camera poses.
Camera poses are adjusted by photometric loss during training to enhance performance instead of using keypoint-metric cost in SfM.
Camera poses are adjusted by the photometric loss during training instead of the keypoint-metric cost in SfM.
Despite its promising results, BARF and its follow-up works still require the pre-computed camera poses from SfM.
One other issue with vanilla NeRF is that it needs time-consuming per-scene training.
Making NeRF generalizable across scenes has recently gained increasing attention.
However, similar to vanilla NeRF, GeNeRFs also depend on accurate camera poses.
There is no existing work that tried to optimize the camera poses jointly with GeNeRFs.
This intrigues us to investigate the replacement of NeRF with GeNeRFs in BARF.
We find that the joint optimization is non-trivial in our task settings, and the camera poses can diverge quickly even when initialized with the ground truths.
In this paper, we identified two potential reasons which cause the failure of bundle adjusting GeNeRFs.
The first reason is the aggregated feature outliers, which are caused by occlusions.
The other reason is due to the high non-convexity of the cost function produced by ResNet features, which produces incoherent displacements.
The issue with incoherent displacements is similar to the issue caused by positional encodings in BARF.
We further proposed our method DBARF, which jointly optimizes GeNeRF and relative camera poses by a deep neural network.

GeNeRF is deep neural network that mizes with relative camera poses to optimize camera pose correction and 3D point warping onto feature maps. The training objective in BARF is equivalent to the implicit training objective in Optimize the relative camera poses from target view in DBARF.
The camera poses are optimized in DBARF alongside GeNeRF without relying on absolute camera poses. Joint optimization of camera poses with GeNeRF is challenging, as shown in the experiment.

DBARF is a method for deep bundle adjusting camera poses with GeNeRFs. It is trained end-to-end without known absolute camera poses. DBARF's generalization is demonstrated to outperform BARF and GARF without per-scene fine-tuning.

DBARF deep bundle adjusting camera poses with GeNeRFs, optimizing camera poses without requiring known absolute camera poses. Generalization ability of DBARF is shown to be superior to both BARF and GARF even without per-scene fine-tuning.

Simply adopting Eq. (10) to optimize the camera poses without knowing the outlier distribution to apply a suitable robust loss ρ(·) can give bad results.
First-order optimizers can also easily get stuck at bad local minima in our task.
Therefore, we seek an approach that can minimize Eq. (10) while bypassing direct gradient descent.
Instead of explicitly taking Eq. (10) as an objective and optimizing camera poses by gradient descent, we implicitly minimize it by taking the feature error as an input to another neural network.
Since NeRF randomly samples points in the target view during training, we lose the spatial information of the features when the neural network directly takes Eq. (10) as input.
